ADJ.etymology: From úlíkr, from *ungalīkaz. lik. Compare unlike, ongelijk, ólíkur, ungleich, ulik.
From røþer, from rauðr, from *raudaz, from *h₁rowdʰós < *h₁rewdʰ-.

NOUN.etymology: krîch. Unlike and krig, the Swedish word has neutrum gender like kríggj.
From vika, from vika, from *wikǭ, from *weyg-.
From fall, from falla. The grammatical sense is a calque of Latin casus.
From mānadagher, from mánadagr, from *mānini dag, a calque of dies lunae, equivalent of dag. Cognate with mandag, måndag, mandag, mánudagur, mánadagur, maanantai, Monday, Montag and maandag.
sǫk, from *sakō. Cognate with sak, sak, sag, sök, sake, zaak, Saak, Sook, Sache. An unrelated word that also underwent the transformation in meaning from "legal matter" to "thing, item" is causa.
From ar, from ár, from *jērą, from *yeh₁r-.
From beslut, from beslut, a besluten. 1513. Compare besluit, Beschluss.
From fōlk, from Runic Swedish fulk, from fólk, folk, from *fulką.
From persona. the late 14th century. persona.
From stormber, from stormr, from *sturmaz, from *(s)twerH-.
From syn, from sýn, sjón, from *siuniz.
From væruld, værald, værild, værld, from verǫld, from *weraldiz. In (intensifier), perhaps elliptical for "världens största" (the world's largest) or the like.
From dumber, from dumbr, from *dumbaz, from *dʰewbʰ-. Compare dumb, dum, dumbur, dumb, dom and dumm.
From dygn, dyghn, døgn, døghn, from dœgn, from *dōgin-, alternative form of *dōg-. mid-14th century.SO}} Cognate with døgn, døgn and døgn. See also dœgr, døger and dægur.
From klockæ, from klokka, from clocca, probably of - origin, from *klokkos (compare cloch, clog), from *klēg-, *klōg-.
Borrowed from critique, from criticus, from κριτικός.
From quæld, qvælder, from kveld, from *kwildiz, from *gʷelH-.
From linia, from linea, from līnum, from *līno-.
From maþer, mander, from maðr, from *mann-.

Known from midhel, middel, medhel, from middel, from middil, from *midlą. Same as German Mittel and English middle. The stand-alone adjective is archaic, but lives on as the superlative medelst, as a prefix medel- and ma
From mánaðr, from *mēnōþs, from a derivative of *mḗh₁n̥s. See also måne.

Since 1661, through sv of the 2nd plural verb suffix ''-(e)n'' and the older pronoun I ("ye"), e.g. ''visste'''n''' I'' > ''visste '''n'''i'' (“did you know”). Compare þér and þið which developed similarly. The i, ir der
From oþinsdagher, from óðinsdagr, from *Wōdanas dag.
From raust, ultimately from a - derivative of *h₃reyH-.922 Related to *runsiz. Cognate with røst.
From spil, from spil and spil, from *spil.
From sikerhet, from sekerheit, akin to het.
From ūtganger, from útgangr, from *ūtgangaz. Equivalent to gång.
From villr, from *wilþijaz. Doublet of vild, which is influenced from Middle Low German.

PRON.etymology: From maþer, mander, from maðr, from *mann-.

Since 1661, through sv of the 2nd plural verb suffix ''-(e)n'' and the older pronoun I ("ye"), e.g. ''visste'''n''' I'' > ''visste '''n'''i'' (“did you know”). Compare þér and þið which developed similarly. The i, ir der
